title: Brokers flag first-home buyer loan slump
full_text:
One of the country’s largest mortgage brokers says the number of prospective first-home buyers applying for loans has plummeted by 20 per cent since Jim Chalmers unveiled his controversial budget, raising questions about Labor’s goal of using the negative gearing and capital gains changes to get young Australians on to the property ladder.
Aussie Home Loans said it had seen first-home buyer lodgements decline more than 20 per cent since Labor unveiled the tax changes in the budget.
This was softer than the more than 25 per cent decline among investors, but the new figures cast into doubt Labor’s tax changes the party said would level the playing field for first-home buyers.
And other mortgage brokers said they had not seen an increase in the number of prospective first-home buyers since the tax changes, with one saying there had been a decrease.
But on Thursday, Anthony Albanese continued to claim success, saying his government’s policies were improving conditions for first-home buyers.
In question time, he went through anecdotal accounts from around the country of people buying their first home and declared, “right around Australia, this is what is happening and this is what will continue to happen”.
Angus Taylor told The Australian the mortgage figures exposed “another Labor lie”.
“Labor has delivered the housing trifecta of failure: fewer homes, higher rents and confidence in the market smashed,” the Opposition Leader said.
Lendi Group – which operates Aussie Home Loans – attributed the more than 20 per cent decline to the tax changes.
“Our data shows first-home buyers have significantly retreated from the market,” chief executive Sebastian Watkins said. “Whilst not at the same levels as investors, the pullback in demand has been more than 20 per cent.
“After six weeks of record low clearance rates and low consumer sentiment, it’s not a surprise.
“When we speak with our customers, they are telling us it’s a combination of three things: uncertainty on new budget settings, affordability and borrowing power after recent rate rises, and exhaustion from the ever-changing macro environment.” Aussie Home Loans also said it had seen first-home buyers “continue to borrow larger amounts”.
Alcove Mortgage Brokers founder Chris Bates said he had seen a “slight decline” in new first-home buyer inquiries since Labor announced the housing tax changes and unleashed a “ridiculous amount of nervousness in the market”.
“You would think there would be an increase in first-time buyers since May,” he said. “It’s probably been a decrease.
“I think a lot of the first-time buyers are very nervous.
“There’s less competition, but they’re more worried about falling prices.” Intelligent Finance mortgage broker Ari Bernstein told The Australian the number of pre-approvals issued for first-home buyers had not changed largely since before the budget.
“There hasn’t been a drop-off in terms of the number of first-time buyer pre-approvals that we’ve submitted post-budget compared to pre-budget,” he said.
But contrary to Mr Bates, he said he had seen a higher rate of prospective first-home buyer inquiries than before. “A lot of these investors … they’ve pulled out of that space in established property because they’re going to lose those tax benefits in the future,” he said.
“So that really leaves the door open for first-time buyers to come into the market with less competition, which is obviously what some of these budget changes were designed for.
“But then at the same time, it’s a unique time in the Australian lending market because interest rates are so high … there’s uncertainty in the market.” The fresh figures add to doubt about the government’s tax changes. The Australian revealed earlier in the week that some of the most popular suburbs for the federal first-home buyer guarantee scheme had seen property values fall and, hence, recent first-home buyers risked negative equity. Amid a downturn in property values more broadly in capital cities, the Reserve Bank’s rate-setting board this week noted “conditions in the housing market had eased by more than expected, reflecting … tax changes announced in the Australian government budget”, as well as increases in the cash rate.
